cue: Say you're starving / dying of hunger (figuratively)

es.meaning: To be starving (figurative); the reflexive marks the hyperbole.
es.example: ¿Ya vamos a comer? Me muero de hambre.
es.exampleTranslation: Are we eating soon? I'm starving.
es.regionNote: Non-reflexive 'morir de hambre' exists and leans LITERAL (famine, starvation). The -se is doing the figurative work — that contrast is the entry's point.

it.meaning: To be starving (figurative) — or literally to starve; context decides.
it.exampleTranslation: When are we eating? I'm starving.

bridge.note: Both die of hunger daily and survive — but Spanish has a switch Italian lacks: the reflexive. Morirse (with -se) is the hyperbole; plain morir leans literal. Italian's morire di fame does both jobs and lets context sort it out. The pattern generalizes across the whole family — me muero de risa / de sueño / de ganas — and Italian answers with a preposition twist of its own: morire DAL ridere, DALLA voglia (da + article for the cause), not always di.

interference.es_to_it: Importing the reflexive gives 'mi muoio di fame' — not Italian; the verb stands alone: muoio di fame. And the family shifts preposition: morire DAL ridere, not 'di ridere'.
interference.it_to_es: Dropping the -se gives 'muero de hambre' — grammatical but oddly grave, like an actual famine report. Everyday hunger wants me muero.

review.notes: Submitted by Joe 2026-07-17. The morirse/morir figurative-literal split and the dal/di cause-preposition claim are Claude's analysis — both reviewers to confirm; the ES side especially (is bare 'muero de hambre' truly marked, or regionally fine?).
